hypothesis: A clean SGDFNet-owned delta baseline can establish the 2025 rolling-month reference under Protocol B.
reason: J0 pass: Protocol B baseline runs end-to-end, clears the overall full-year RT SMAPE target, and becomes the accepted comparison point for future branches.
next_action: Advance to J1 feature redesign with focus on reducing 9-16 RT SMAPE.

hypothesis: Adding weekly-history lag features may improve recurring regime recall for 9-16 and tail behavior.
reason: Rejected under J1: weekly-history additions regressed both full-year raw RT SMAPE and segment 9-16 RT SMAPE relative to the accepted J0 baseline, while tail metrics also worsened.
next_action: Switch to a different J1 feature family; do not micro-tune weekly-history lags.

hypothesis: Historical forecast-residual features may provide leakage-safe bias-correction signal for RT delta, especially in the difficult 9-16 block.
reason: Kept under J1 because both primary raw metrics improved relative to the accepted J0 baseline. Gains are small and capped/tail metrics are mixed, so this is an incremental baseline upgrade rather than a decisive feature win.
next_action: Use the residual-history baseline as the new comparison point and test a different J1 feature family next.

hypothesis: Same-hour cross-day rolling stats may sharpen segment-local regime memory, especially around the 9-16 block.
reason: Rejected under J1: segment-local rolling stats improved positive-direction recall slightly but regressed both primary raw metrics and worsened 9-16 performance materially versus the accepted residual-history baseline.
next_action: Switch to a different J1 family; do not tune segment-local rolling-stat variants further.

hypothesis: Forecast pressure interaction features may expose supply-demand stress and improve difficult 9-16 price deviations.
reason: Rejected under J1: forecast-pressure interactions improved direction diagnostics slightly but still regressed both primary raw metrics relative to the accepted residual-history baseline.
next_action: J1 feature-family search is exhausted for now; switch to J2 strong-baseline objective changes.

hypothesis: Upweighting train-tail delta samples may improve hard-regime price errors enough to justify a stronger baseline objective.
reason: Rejected under J2: tail weighting delivered a strong tail win but materially worsened both primary raw metrics and the 9-16 segment metric versus the accepted residual-history baseline.
next_action: Keep the tail-weighting evidence as a diagnostic, but switch to a different J2 baseline mechanism.

hypothesis: Training separate regressors by segment may stop easy segments from dominating the difficult 9-16 block.
reason: Rejected under J2: segment-conditioned regressors degraded every major metric, including overall RT SMAPE, 9-16 RT SMAPE, capped metrics, and tail metrics.
next_action: Do not continue segment-conditioned variants in this form; checkpoint J2 and switch to a different objective family.

hypothesis: A mild asymmetric quantile objective may improve difficult positive-deviation behavior without the collateral damage of global tail weighting.
reason: Rejected under J2: asymmetric quantile loss sharply improved positive-direction recall but still regressed both primary raw metrics, the 9-16 segment metric, capped metrics, and tail metrics relative to the accepted residual-history baseline.
next_action: Do not continue asymmetric-loss variants in this form; switch to a validation-calibrated post-fit repair branch.

hypothesis: A tiny validation-only 9-16 segment bias correction may recover price metrics without the collateral damage seen in objective-level changes.
reason: Kept under J2 because the validation-only 9-16 bias calibration improved both primary raw metrics and both capped metrics relative to the accepted residual-history baseline, while also improving positive-direction recall. Tail metrics were effectively flat-to-slightly worse, so this is a modest but valid baseline upgrade.
next_action: Use the val-segment-bias baseline as the new anchor and test a more targeted validation-calibrated direction repair next.

hypothesis: If validation-only bias correction is real signal rather than luck, extending it to all segments might preserve the 9-16 gain and improve the rest of the day too.
reason: Rejected under J2: extending validation-only bias calibration to all segments held the 9-16 metric flat but gave back overall raw and capped SMAPE relative to the accepted 9-16-only bias baseline. Tail metrics improved only trivially and do not outweigh the primary regression.
next_action: Keep the 9-16-only bias baseline as the accepted anchor and move to a more targeted direction-calibration branch.

hypothesis: Within the 9-16 block, separate validation-only bias estimates for predicted-positive and predicted-nonpositive deltas may improve directional behavior without giving up the accepted 9-16 bias gain.
reason: Rejected under J2: direction-aware 9-16 calibration improved tail metrics slightly, but it regressed both primary raw metrics and both capped metrics relative to the accepted 9-16-only bias baseline.
next_action: Keep the simpler 9-16-only bias baseline and move to threshold sensitivity or another narrow validation-side repair.

hypothesis: If the accepted 9-16 bias gain comes mainly from larger predicted deviations, applying the correction only above a validation-derived magnitude threshold may keep the win while reducing collateral drift.
reason: Rejected under J2: thresholding the 9-16 calibration slightly helped tail metrics, but regressed both primary raw metrics and both capped metrics relative to the accepted full 9-16 bias baseline.
next_action: Keep the simpler full 9-16 bias baseline and move to another narrow validation-side sensitivity branch.

hypothesis: If regime drift inside the validation month matters, using only the most recent validation days for 9-16 bias estimation may sharpen the accepted calibration.
reason: Rejected under J2: restricting the 9-16 bias estimate to the most recent validation week materially worsened both primary raw metrics and both capped metrics relative to the accepted full-validation 9-16 bias baseline.
next_action: Keep the full-validation 9-16 bias baseline and move to another narrow calibration sensitivity branch.

hypothesis: If the accepted 9-16 calibration is really about average residual shift rather than robust center shift, replacing the median bias with a mean bias may improve the repair.
reason: Rejected under J2: switching the 9-16 calibration reducer from median to mean caused a broad collapse in primary raw metrics, capped metrics, and direction accuracy. Tail metrics improved slightly, but the tradeoff is far too severe.
next_action: Keep the median-based 9-16 bias baseline and do not continue mean-based calibration in this family.

hypothesis: If the accepted 9-16 median bias is compatible with only a very mild tail emphasis, a small tail weight may recover some tail error without sacrificing the primary hierarchy.
changed_factor: Validation-only 9-16 bias plus mild tail weighting
reason: Rejected under J2: even a mild tail weight combined with the accepted 9-16 bias repair materially regressed both primary raw metrics and both capped metrics, despite yielding clear top-tail gains.
next_action: Preserve the pure median 9-16 bias baseline as the accepted anchor and treat bias-plus-tail as a diagnostic rather than a release candidate.

hypothesis: If the accepted 9-16 calibration should lean slightly toward one side of the residual distribution rather than the exact median, a q=0.40 residual bias may outperform the median-based repair.
reason: Rejected under J2: moving the 9-16 calibration reducer from the median to a q=0.40 residual quantile materially worsened both primary raw metrics, both capped metrics, and directional metrics relative to the accepted median-based baseline.
next_action: Keep the median-based 9-16 bias baseline and treat residual-quantile skewing as exhausted for now.
